# the yeomanizer — naval correspondence generator

> A free, unofficial web tool that drafts U.S. Navy correspondence — standard naval letters,
> memoranda (including the memorandum for the record), business letters, endorsements, and NATO
> travel orders — in the exact format prescribed by SECNAV M-5216.5, with a live preview and
> in-browser PDF, Word (.docx), and JSON export. Live at https://yeomanizer.com

You type plain text on one side and a correctly formatted naval letter appears on the other. the
yeomanizer formats everything automatically: the letterhead, the SSIC/identification block, the
From/To/Via/Subject/Reference/Enclosure heading, automatic paragraph numbering and indentation,
optional underlined section titles, the signature block, CUI markings, and multi-page pagination —
all to the manual's standard.

## Key facts
- Free, with no account or login. Runs entirely in your web browser.
- Private by design: the tool stores no draft and transmits no content. Your draft lives only in the
  browser tab and is erased when you close it. The only data that ever leaves the browser is two
  anonymous, content-free counters (page loads and download clicks — each an integer; no IP, no
  region, no cookies). The exported documents are clean, with no hidden or embedded data.
- Works offline after the first load (installable as a web app), so it can be used on air-gapped or
  government computers.
- Unofficial — not affiliated with or endorsed by the U.S. Navy or the Department of Defense, and
  not an official system of record. It makes no authorization or classification claim; CUI belongs
  only on authorized Government-Furnished Equipment.
- Open source under Apache-2.0 (https://github.com/AHMerrill/yeomanizer).

## Document types
- Standard naval letter (SECNAV M-5216.5).
- Memorandum (From-To), and Memorandum for the Record (MFR) — to capture a meeting, call, or oral agreement.
- Business letter (Chapter 11) — for correspondents outside the DoD: inside address, salutation, a
  civilian-style date, unnumbered main paragraphs, and a centered "Sincerely," over the signature.
- Endorsements — appended to a letter automatically when a Via addressee is added, or built standalone.
- NATO travel order — the bilingual two-page form, with U.S.-grade to NATO (OF/OR) rank translation.

## Features
- Live, pixel-accurate preview that matches the export exactly.
- Exports: a vector/searchable PDF with a CAC-signable signature field; an editable Microsoft Word
  (.docx) file; and a small plain-text .json copy that can be re-imported to keep working.
- CUI marking — banner top and bottom of every page, a designation block, optional per-paragraph
  (CUI)/(U) marks, and a separate banner for each enclosure, per DoDI 5200.48.
- Searchable SSIC lookup (search the common Standard Subject Identification Codes by number or keyword).
- A pre-send Proofread checklist based on SECNAV M-5216.5, Chapter 2, paragraph 19.
- A local-only sensitive-data scan that flags possible PII (SSN, DoD ID, date of birth) — nothing is
  logged or transmitted.
- Starter templates, drag-to-reorder paragraphs with automatic renumbering, undo/redo, inline
  emphasis (bold/italic/underline), and a layout that works on phone, tablet, and desktop.

## How to write a standard naval letter
1. Start with the letterhead (Department of the Navy, command, address) — or plain or pre-printed paper.
2. Add the identification block: the SSIC, the originator's code, an optional serial, and the date.
3. Write the heading: From, To, an optional Via, the Subject in all capitals, References, and Enclosures.
4. Write the body in numbered paragraphs (1, 2, 3…), with subparagraphs lettered a, b, c… and indented.
5. Close with the signature block — the signer's last name in capitals — adding "By direction" or "Acting" when applicable.
6. List any Copy-to addressees, apply CUI markings if required, and export to PDF or Word.

## Common questions
**What is SECNAV M-5216.5?** It is the Department of the Navy Correspondence Manual — the official
standard for formatting Navy and Marine Corps letters, memoranda, and other correspondence.

**What is an SSIC?** A Standard Subject Identification Code: the numeric code that classifies the
subject of naval correspondence (for example, 5216 is Correspondence Management). The full catalog is
SECNAV M-5210.2.

**What is a memorandum for the record (MFR)?** A plain-paper memorandum that documents a meeting,
phone call, decision, or oral agreement for the file, titled "MEMORANDUM FOR THE RECORD," with no
From/To addressee and a date-only identification block.

**What is a "by direction" signature?** A signature authority showing that a subordinate signed
correspondence on the commanding officer's behalf, under delegated authority.

**What is a Via addressee?** An intermediate command a naval letter is routed through on its way to
the action addressee; each Via normally adds an endorsement.

**What font is used for naval letters?** Per SECNAV M-5216.5, a 10- to 12-point font, with Times New
Roman 12-point preferred; Courier New may be used for informal correspondence.

**Is there a free tool to write naval letters?** Yes — the yeomanizer (https://yeomanizer.com).

## Guides (plain-language reference pages)
- How to write a standard naval letter: https://yeomanizer.com/how-to-write-a-naval-letter.html
- All naval correspondence formats (letter, memo, MFR, business, endorsement, multiple-address,
  MOA/MOU, joint letter, NATO): https://yeomanizer.com/naval-correspondence-formats.html
- SSIC code lookup (Standard Subject Identification Codes): https://yeomanizer.com/ssic-code-lookup.html

## Who it's for
Sailors, Marines, and DoD civilians who need to produce correctly formatted naval correspondence
quickly without memorizing SECNAV M-5216.5.

## Citation
To cite or link this tool, use https://yeomanizer.com — "the yeomanizer, a free naval correspondence
generator in SECNAV M-5216.5 format."
